text: This is a case of a seventeen-year-old boy presenting with complaints of perspiration and palpitation. He was found to have a five point five centimeter right supranial mass. His metaneprines were elevated. After adequate preparation, he was taken up for robotic assisted laparoscopic adjuvant treatment. Patient was positioned with right side up and three robotic ports were planned along with one assistant port and another port for liver retraction.

The initial laparoscopic view shows a highly vascular tumor. The surgeon drops the colon medially and develops a plane between the inferior vena cava and the tumor. The hepatic ligaments are cut to mobilize the liver superiorly, with a retractor placed to maintain its position. A harmonic scalpel is used to manage bleeding from peritumoral vessels during dissection.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to expose and manage the tumoral vessels. The plane between the upper pole of the kidney and the tumor is developed, revealing additional vascular structures. Hemlock clips are applied to these vessels on both sides before they are divided. Dissection continues between the upper pole of the kidney and the suprarenal mass.

The surgeon lifts the tumor from its bed using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, exposing the swath muscle. Medial connections of the tumor with the inferior vena cava are clipped and divided, and all veins draining into the inferior vena cava from the tumor are similarly clipped and divided using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon exposes normal adrenal tissue at the superior extent of the tumor. Scissors are introduced to perform further dissection due to the non-articulating nature of the harmonic scalpel. The tumor is completely mobilized off its bed, along with the normal adrenal tissue, and the final peritoneal attachments are severed, freeing the tumor.
The surgical specimen is sealed in a retrieval bag and extracted through a small incision, with minimal changes in blood pressure observed during the process.
